vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. You already learned that 出る means "to exit," and this is the transitive version of that. In other words, you're doing the action of causing something else to exit, so this word means to take out. When you take something out, you're making it exit where it previously was, right? 出す is a super versatile word that can also mean to turn in (an assignment, for example), to put out something (like the garbage or recycling), or to let out something (a sigh, a sound, or even a bodily fluid). It can also be a casual term for paying for something, since you're taking out your cash and handing it over.

Your dad's okay with this, although you sometimes cause him <vocabulary>to let out</vocabulary> a wail when he sees his monthly statement. Thanks, Daddy — love you!

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
This word means <vocabulary>to understand</vocabulary>, which might surprise you if you remember that the kanji 分 means <kanji>part</kanji>. Think of it this way: to understand something you have to break it down into its parts to see how it works.

Watch out, though, because whereas in English you understand something, in Japanese the thing you understand is the grammatical subject (it's doing the action). So, a more literal meaning for this is <vocabulary>to be understandable</vocabulary>. In other words, there's no object, and 分かる is intransitive, meaning it happens on its own. The かる ending is a hint, because verbs with かる as okurigana (kana added to the kanji) are usually intransitive.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
The 々 character just repeats the kanji before it. In other words: <kanji>color</kanji>, <kanji>color</kanji>! But think figuratively, here — something with lots of different colors like this could also be described as <vocabulary>various</vocabulary>, which is exactly what this word means. 

色々 used to mean a variety of colors, but over time evolved into a more general word for a diverse variety of something. You'll often see it as an adjective, like 色々な人 (various people), but it also pops up when talking about mixed experiences or actions, as in 色々あったね (a lot happened) or 色々考えた (I thought about it in all kinds of ways). Kind of appropriate how this word has various uses, eh?
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading for this kanji is just the kun'yomi reading twice. Since you've learned the kun'yomi reading already, you should be able to read this word as well. Nothing tricky about it.

context_sentences.en: Please give me some pink wool.
context_sentences.ja: ピンク色の毛糸を下さい。

context_sentences.en: Grandma gave me a wool blanket she made herself.
context_sentences.ja: おばあちゃんが自分で作った毛糸のブランケットをくれました。

context_sentences.en: These jeans are actually made of wool.
context_sentences.ja: このジーンズ、本当は毛糸でできています。

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: You'll have to learn this word in two parts. You already know the meaning of 今 because of the kanji, which means "now." The すぐ part, however, is a new word, meaning "soon." すぐ and "soon" even start with a similar sound, so maybe that can help you remember this! If you put 今 and すぐ together, you get "now soon." Put in better English, that's <vocabulary>at once</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>right now</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: Since you already know the vocab word 今 (read いま), you should be able to read this word as well.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
This is the village you came from. This is where your parents live. This is your <vocabulary>hometown</vocabulary> (same meaning as the kanji you learned).

This word is old-fashioned and evokes a warm, nostalgic image of home. Often seen in folktales and traditional songs, it represents an idyllic, pastoral life.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as with the kanji you learned, so you should feel nice and "at home" with this one 😉

context_sentences.ja: 十六才で里から出て、ウェブデザイナーになりました。

context_sentences.en: I went down to the village to get some water.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
You have your <kanji>home village</kanji> and your <kanji>heart</kanji>. Your heart always resides in your home village, so when you leave you feel <vocabulary>homesick</vocabulary>.

Young people don't really use this word anymore. It's more for the oldies. If you're an oldie, go right ahead. If you're a youngie, then talk to more oldies!
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The readings are double kun'yomi. The kun'yomi reading is what you learned with 里 already, though you'll have to pull out the 心 vocab reading to remember it all. Not only that, the 心 gets rendaku'd here making it ごころ. Make sure you watch out for that.
